artifacts.content:
Hi Dad,

Just grabbing ten quiet minutes before service to finally answer your last message. Yes, things are still full-on here but we’re okay, promise.

I wanted to tell you something properly rather than you hearing it in dribs and drabs from neighbours. Back in March 2021, when we were still running the community meal hub out of The Green Fork’s kitchen, I used £4,800 from what Mum left me after she died at Bassetlaw District General in January 2020. The pay-what-you-can pot had run dry and some of the Ryton Estate regulars were coming in in tears about choosing between food and rent arrears. I couldn’t stomach turning them away, so I quietly topped things up from the inheritance. It felt like the sort of thing she’d have wanted me to do with it.

It did mean I had to juggle my own bills. I even extended my NatWest overdraft by £12k in November 2020 to keep paying the Plot to Plate allotment growers on time so they could heat their greenhouses that winter. That’s why I ended up two months behind on council tax at 27 Beechwood Terrace and got that stroppy arrears letter from Bassetlaw District Council in Feb 2021. Not my finest financial planning, but we got through it.

Day-to-day, the bistro is a bit quieter this year than I’d like, which is stressful, but the regulars are still brilliant. I’ve even scribbled “sort out Instagram” in my little planning notebook because I know I should be shouting more about what we’re doing, but you know me – I barely touch the bistro’s Facebook page as it is and I’ve never really got the hang of posting regular updates. Mum would have loved watching people tucking into Jacob’s favourite mac and cheese or Amelia smearing roast carrots everywhere.

Anyway, I’ll pop round next Sunday after dog walk if you’re in. I’ll bring some leftover tart.

Love,
Em

Ollie:  
You alive? Kids down yet? Just finished wiping aioli off the pass.

Emma:  
Barely! Double sitting tonight. How are J & A? Mum says Jacob’s obsessed with dinosaurs now.

Ollie:  
Yeah, he’s renamed Molly “Triceratops” 🙄  
How’s your head? You sounded wiped on Sunday.

Emma:  
Bit better. Still get wobbly sometimes thinking about that Friday last May when I ended up locked in the staff loo at The Green Fork having a full-blown panic attack mid-service. Dr. Patel at Newgate called it generalised anxiety and stuck me on 50mg sertraline. Glamorous life.

Ollie:  
Bloody hell, Em. You should’ve told me sooner.

Emma:  
Didn’t want to worry you after everything in Oct 2022 when Wilko cut your hours at the distribution centre and you admitted you’d been skipping meals so Jacob and Amelia could eat. I still think about that night. I’m glad we got you all coming to Community Table on Thursdays and Laura just puts it through as staff meals at £0 so it doesn’t make a big thing of it. Kids just think they’re getting a treat at Auntie Em’s.

Ollie:  
You literally kept us afloat, you know that?

Emma:  
You’d do the same. Anyway, the sertraline plus the new kitchen rules – 10‑minute breaks every two hours, no shouting, full stop – have helped loads. Whole team’s calmer.

Ollie:  
Proud of you. Also, can we book in Sunday lunch at yours? Rachel’s craving your roast potatoes.

Emma:  
Deal. I’ll do pork from Moss Valley and something green so we all pretend to be healthy.

Title: Random brain dump – things to sort

– Social media: I need to get my act together. The Bistro’s Insta (@greenforkbistro) does okay when I actually post, but in reality I go weeks without putting anything up and the Facebook page is basically just occasional event posters. I’ve never really got into doing regular updates about the community stuff we’re proud of, which feels like a missed trick when bookings are quieter.

– Press: still twitchy about the Worksop Guardian piece from 12 Nov 2020 – “Local Bistro Shut Amid Vomiting Bug Fears”. Environmental Health from Bassetlaw District Council cleared us completely after that two‑week closure, but the headline still pops up if you Google “Emma Cartwright Worksop”. Must remember: it hurt, but we survived and standards are solid.

– Staff wellbeing: keep building on the changes since Dr. Patel at Newgate diagnosed GAD and started me on 50mg sertraline after that horrible panic attack in the staff loo mid‑service (May 2023). The 10‑minute break rule every two hours is working; maybe add a rota’d “fresh air” break on Saturdays.

– Private Insta (@emma.cartwright.worksop): think about whether to keep posting about the walk‑in fridge panic attacks from early 2022 and the 999 night when James collapsed on 14 Jan 2022, or archive some Stories so it doesn’t feel like I’m stuck in that moment.

– Future idea: short video series on low‑waste cooking + mental health in kitchens – honest but not oversharing.

Subject: Quick life/work ramble while I drink this tea

Hey Rach,

Sitting at the kitchen table at Beechwood with a lukewarm peppermint tea while Molly snores, so you’re getting a rambling update.

Work first: the bistro’s okay but not exactly booming – this year’s been a bit softer and I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t hoping for anything that might nudge bookings up. I keep telling myself word of mouth and the regulars are what matter, but the gas bill disagrees.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how far we’ve come since the chaotic first year. Do you remember when I had to cancel that whole weekend of bookings because I’d overpromised on a wedding tasting and couldn’t get enough staff? I still cringe thinking about phoning regulars to apologise. Learned the hard way about rotas and saying no.

On the plus side, we’ve had some lovely recognition – that Bassetlaw Green Business Award last year, the BBC Radio Nottingham chat, and the usual nice TripAdvisor comments. No shiny national awards or anything, but customers telling us it feels like “their place” is better anyway.

Home front: Daniel’s buried in mock exam marking. We’ve had a few more of those slightly scary money chats – not rows, just the “how long can we both work like this?” conversation. He’s gently raised the whole kids question again. I’ve started training my sous to take more of the evening services so I can at least imagine stepping back a bit one day.

Anyway, enough navel‑gazing. How are you? How’s work? Send me a picture of Amelia in that fox jumper, please.

Love,
Em

Subject: Allergy incident – follow-up and next steps

Hi Sarah,

Hope you’re well. I wanted to update you properly about the nut allergy incident we had back in August 2022 at The Green Fork.

A teenage diner with a documented nut allergy reacted after eating here – it turned out one of our junior chefs had ignored the allergen chart and used a pesto containing traces of nuts. We called an ambulance and they were taken to Bassetlaw Hospital A&E as a precaution. They recovered, thank God, but it shook all of us.

In response, I booked the entire team, including myself, onto an intensive Allergy UK training course in Sheffield on 12–13 September 2022. It cost £1,350 but was worth every penny. We’ve since rewritten our allergen protocols from scratch: separate prep areas, colour‑coded utensils, double sign‑off on every allergy ticket, and a pre‑service briefing before each shift. You know how much I’ve poured into The Green Fork since opening in 2019, especially after that really harsh local blog review in our first months that said the service was slow and the menu was “overly worthy and a bit joyless” – that one absolutely floored me at the time and made me question whether anyone actually got what I was trying to do here on Newcastle Avenue.

It brought back some of the anxiety from earlier that year when James had his breakdown during service on 14 Jan 2022 and I ended up calling 999 from the kitchen at The Green Fork. You remember I started getting panic attacks in the walk‑in after that and ended up doing weekly venting Stories on my private Insta (@emma.cartwright.worksop) and sessions with my therapist at Bassetlaw Mind. All of that has made me more determined to build a kitchen culture that’s calm, careful and kind, rather than high‑octane chaos.

If you’re still happy to, I’d love you to come in one afternoon and do a short refresher session with the team on allergy communication from a dietitian’s perspective.

Best,
Emma Louise Cartwright
Owner‑Chef, The Green Fork Bistro
27 Beechwood Terrace, Worksop

Event: GP follow-up – anxiety / meds review

Date: 2023-06-15  
Time: 09:10–09:30  
Location: Newgate Medical Centre, Worksop – Dr. Patel

Notes:
– Follow-up after the panic attack during that fully booked Friday night service at The Green Fork in May 2023 (ended up in the staff loo unable to breathe properly).  
– Check in on sertraline 50 mg: side effects (sleep a bit weird, jaw tension?), whether dosage is right given the pace of the kitchen.
– Update Dr. P on changes we’ve made at the bistro: mandatory 10‑minute breaks every 2 hours on shift, no‑shouting rule on the pass, clearer rota so people actually get days off.
– Mention that the old panic attacks in the walk‑in fridge after James’s collapse on 14 Jan 2022 have eased since therapy at Bassetlaw Mind, but I still get spikes when we’re short‑staffed.
– Ask about exercise + meds: is it okay that I’m cycling to The Green Fork most days and doing short yoga sessions on the dry‑store mat between lunch and dinner?
– Practical: take list of current meds, leave kitchen prep list with sous, ask Daniel to do morning dog walk along the Chesterfield Canal so I can get to Newgate for 9:10.

Subject: Massive thank you (and a confession!)

Hi Karen,

I’ve been meaning to write this for weeks, but you know what service is like once we hit spring.

I just wanted to say a proper thank you for everything you did for me back in 2019 when The Green Fork was drowning in that £18k PAYE and VAT mess. I still remember sitting in the corner of The Old Mill Café feeling sick while you calmly went through the StepChange paperwork and helped me figure out a plan. Finishing that six‑month debt management course was grim but weirdly one of the best “educations” I’ve had.

When I made the final payment to HMRC in February 2022 I actually cried in the stockroom. I texted Daniel straight away but I should have emailed you then too. I honestly learned more from that whole saga than from any business workshop.

I’m still not perfect on the organisational front – I have a terrible habit of leaving admin to the last minute and I’ve even missed a couple of the Nottinghamshire Independent Hospitality Network meet‑ups because I forgot to put them in the diary. But I’m trying to get better: blocking out a weekly “laptop hour” between lunch and dinner, using the calendar properly instead of scribbles on the back of order sheets.

If you’re ever in Worksop on a quiet Monday, staff meal’s on me. I’d love to show you how far the place has come since those panic‑ridden early days.

Big hug,
Emma

Subject: Quick update – health & weird “qualification”

Hi Mum,

Finally sitting down after lunch service, so I thought I’d send you a proper update instead of rushed WhatsApps.

Do you remember that NHS “Eat Well, Live Well” course I did at Bassetlaw Hospital back in March–April 2018, before the bistro found its feet? Dr. Patel at Kilton Medical Centre referred me because I was stress‑eating my way through whole loaves of bread after double shifts. It was a six‑week group and I had four one‑to‑one sessions with a dietitian called Sarah Milner. She was brilliant – very down‑to‑earth, no faddy stuff.

One of the best bits was when Daniel came along for a joint session and we planned how to make staff meals less of a beige carb‑fest. That’s where the big salad bowls and the “one veg dish everyone has to try” idea came from. Funny how something that felt a bit embarrassing at the time has actually shaped the way we feed the team now.

I still have wobbles with food when things get hectic – especially after that horrible Worksop Guardian headline in November 2020 about the supposed norovirus. Even though Environmental Health cleared us after the two‑week closure, it knocked my confidence and I spent a lot of evenings comfort‑eating on the sofa at 27 Beechwood Terrace with Molly as a hot‑water bottle.

Anyway, I’m a lot kinder to myself these days. Between what I learned on that course and the cycling, dog walks in The Canch, and Daniel’s nagging, I’m doing okay.

Love you,
Em x

From: Emma Cartwright <emma@greenforkbistro.co.uk>
To: Rachel Cartwright <rachel.turner@example.com>
Date: 15 July 2021
Subject: That weird Channel 4 thing

Hey Rach,

Finally grabbing ten quiet minutes after lunch service to fill you in properly.

So on 7 July this Channel 4 producer turned up at The Green Fork Bistro, said she’d been following our Instagram and wanted to talk about a new reality series about “struggling restaurants.” She was very charming, lots of chat about supporting independents after COVID, “authentic stories,” blah blah. I was knackered and flattered and, stupidly, I let her talk me into signing an initial contributor agreement there and then at the pass.

Fast forward a week and they’ve now emailed over a draft shooting schedule and posted a hard copy to the house at 27 Beechwood Terrace. The plan was to film on and off from October–December 2021, both at the Bistro and at home. The bit that’s really rattled me is the notes in the schedule about “evening debriefs” and “late-night discussions with partner Daniel Harper re: money, staff performance, and long-term plans.” When I asked what that meant, the producer basically admitted they’d be pushing for rows for “drama.”

Daniel’s livid (quite rightly) and I just feel sick that I didn’t read the small print before signing anything.

We’ve emailed this afternoon to say we’re not comfortable with the direction and that we’re pulling out of the series completely. I’d rather keep the restaurant genuinely struggling than pretend to scream at Dan on camera about rotas and supplier bills.

Anyway, lesson learned. Please remind me of this next time I get dazzled by TV people.

Love,
Em x

Personal note – for supporting information draft

Box 2: Supporting Information – Including Qualifications, Awards, Press and Social Media

I should probably include the Channel 4 approach here as an example of media interest, even though we ultimately declined. I haven’t actually won any formal awards yet – most of the recognition for The Green Fork Bistro has just been nice comments from regulars, a few good TripAdvisor reviews, and the odd local press mention – so I’m trying to work out what reasonably “counts” for this section.

In 2021 I was approached by a Channel 4 producer at The Green Fork Bistro on 7 July, asking if I’d consider being filmed for a reality series about “struggling restaurants.” At the time we were still recovering from the pandemic pivot, so the idea of national coverage felt tempting. I actually signed an initial contributor agreement at the Bistro that afternoon, without taking it home to think about properly.

A week or so later they sent through a draft shooting schedule to my home address at 27 Beechwood Terrace, covering proposed filming blocks from October–December 2021. The plan wasn’t just to capture service at the restaurant; they specifically wanted to film late-night arguments between me and my partner, Daniel Harper, about money, staff performance, and whether the business was sustainable. Those “domestic conflict” segments were highlighted as key beats for “narrative tension.”

After talking it through with Daniel and reflecting on the impact that kind of exposure could have on the team, my family, and our mental health, we decided to pull out before filming began. I informed the producer in writing that we were withdrawing consent and asked them to destroy any preliminary material.

Although we never went ahead, I think it still demonstrates a level of external recognition for what we’re doing at The Green Fork and my growing profile via social media and local press, even if it’s not the same as a framed award on the wall.

Notes to self:
